rect.data-narration: Hadoop is an open-source, distributed computing framework designed to process large amounts of data across a cluster of computers. It is written in Java and provides a scalable and fault-tolerant way to process big data.

Hadoop was first developed in 2004 by Doug Cutting and Mike Cafarella at the University of California, Berkeley. It was initially called "Nutch," a web crawler, and was later renamed to Hadoop. The first public release of Hadoop was in 2006.

Hadoop is designed to handle large amounts of data and scale horizontally, making it suitable for big data analytics. It supports a wide range of data formats, including text, image, audio, and video. Hadoop is free and open-source, making it an affordable solution for big data analytics. Hadoop provides a simple and intuitive API, making it easy to use for developers and non-technical users.

The Distributed File System (HDFS) stores data across a cluster of computers, making it highly scalable and fault-tolerant. The MapReduce processing framework is used to process data in parallel across a cluster of computers. The YARN (Yet Another Resource Negotiator) manages resources, such as CPU and memory, across the Hadoop cluster.
HDFS stores data in fixed-size blocks, making it efficient for large data sets. It is highly scalable and fault-tolerant, making it suitable for big data analytics.
YARN manages resources, such as CPU and memory, across the Hadoop cluster. It provides a secure way to manage resources, making it suitable for big data analytics.
MapReduce is a programming model used to process data in parallel across a cluster of computers. It is highly scalable and fault-tolerant, making it suitable for big data analytics.
